reaction_name: 1,3-Dipolar cycloaddition of phenylacetylene with ethyl nitroacetate to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate
bond_formed: C–O and C–C bonds in isoxazole ring
bond_broken: N–O bond rearrangement in nitrile oxide generation; loss of H2O from ethyl nitroacetate

feed_streams.reasoning: Dipolarophile dissolved in DES; separate stream to control stoichiometry and mixing

reagents: ethyl nitroacetate (2.0 equiv) in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
feed_streams.reasoning: Nitrile oxide precursor dissolved in DES; 2.0 equiv needed for full conversion; kept separate until mixing point to avoid premature nitrile oxide dimerization at room temperature

reagents.name: phenylacetylene
reagents.notes: Terminal alkyne; reacts regioselectively to give 5-phenylisoxazole

reagents.name: ethyl nitroacetate
reagents.notes: Generates ethoxycarbonyl nitrile oxide in situ via thermal dehydration; excess used to drive conversion

reagents.name: tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB)
reagents.notes: Forms deep eutectic solvent with ethylene glycol; acts as reaction medium promoting nitrile oxide formation

reagents.name: ethylene glycol (EG)
reagents.notes: Hydrogen bond donor in DES; lowers melting point of TBAB

mechanism_steps.description: Thermal tautomerization/dehydration of ethyl nitroacetate to generate ethoxycarbonyl nitrile oxide (ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide) in situ. The DES medium facilitates this transformation through hydrogen-bonding activation.

Regioselective [3+2] cycloaddition of ethoxycarbonyl nitrile oxide (1,3-dipole) with phenylacetylene (dipolarophile) to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate. The phenyl group directs to the 5-position due to steric and electronic effects.

stream_logic.reasoning: Dipolarophile in DES; kept separate from nitrile oxide precursor to ensure controlled mixing at the T-mixer entrance to the heated zone, minimizing nitrile oxide dimerization
Nitrile oxide precursor in DES; 2.0 equiv relative to phenylacetylene; separate feed allows precise stoichiometric control via flow rate ratio

mixing_order_reasoning: Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate should be mixed at a T-mixer immediately before entering the heated coil reactor. This ensures the nitrile oxide generated thermally is immediately trapped by the dipolarophile, minimizing competitive dimerization to furoxan. The DES is viscous (~50-100 cP at RT) so preheating feed lines to ~60°C may improve pumpability and mixing quality.
deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species; the reaction proceeds under air in batch.
quench_reasoning: The batch protocol quenches with water for workup purposes only; in flow, the product stream can be collected directly and worked up offline. No reactive intermediates persist after the heated zone.
wavelength_reasoning: This is a purely thermal reaction; no photocatalyst or photochemical activation is involved.
confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism (well-established nitrile oxide [3+2] cycloaddition). The DES viscosity is a practical concern for flow — TBAB/EG (1:5) is moderately viscous at room temperature (~50-100 cP) but becomes much less viscous at 120°C (~5-10 cP). Feed lines may need preheating to 50-60°C for reliable pumping. Syringe pumps or HPLC pumps with heated heads recommended. The exact mechanism of nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate in DES (whether purely thermal dehydration or DES-mediated) is not fully established but the overall transformation is well-documented. The 2.0 equiv of ethyl nitroacetate suggests some decomposition/dimerization of the nitrile oxide occurs; flow may improve this ratio.

intensification_mandate.flow_justification_basis: Flow provides three key advantages: (1) Rapid, uniform heating of viscous DES in narrow-bore tubing (high surface-to-volume ratio ~4000 m⁻¹ for 1mm ID) enables precise 120°C temperature control, reducing thermal gradients that cause nitrile oxide dimerization. (2) The 15 min batch reaction can potentially be reduced to ~5-7 min with superheated conditions (back-pressure regulator allowing >120°C) and improved heat transfer. (3) Continuous operation eliminates batch turnaround time, increasing throughput. The transient nitrile oxide intermediate benefits from the plug-flow concentration profile where it is immediately consumed by excess dipolarophile.
